transcript:
Right now you're watching a square frame of orderly little dashes dissolve — they break loose and start drifting on their own paths, and wherever two of them cross, a little burst of warm light flickers into being and then vanishes.

Here's the thing worth noticing: look in the code where the piece defines its objects — they're not called particles or dots, they're called "influences," moving along a "bearing," bent by things called "shapers." That's deliberate. The comment right above them says it outright: "no particle exists until relation creates it." And then further down, in the render function, there's a whole grid — the crossing field — that never stores a single shape. It just accumulates a density number every time a trajectory passes through a cell. The glowing amber nodes you see aren't objects being drawn — they're the code checking "did enough lines cross here?" and only then painting something into existence.

So nothing in this piece "is." Every glow you're seeing is really just a tally of past crossings, computed fresh every frame, forgotten the moment the paths move on.
